findings.lessonTitle: Boolean Logic: The Language of Yes and No

issues.description: starter.py file content ('# TODO: Implement this') doesn't match challenge.json starterCode field ('# TODO: Add your implementation here'). Inconsistency between the two sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Align starter.py content with challenge.json starterCode or vice versa.

issues.description: content/01-content.md is a placeholder stub: 'This is a sufficiently long piece of placeholder text designed to meet the minimum length requirement of fifty characters for a lesson's content section.' Contains no real educational content.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove 01-content.md (since 01-theory.md already exists and serves as the intro theory) or replace with real content.

issues.description: There are two files with '01-' prefix: 01-content.md (stub) and 01-theory.md (real content). This creates ambiguity about rendering order.

content/01-theory.md is a placeholder stub: 'This is a sufficiently long piece of placeholder text designed to meet the minimum length requirement of fifty characters for a lesson's content section. It has proper frontmatter so the loader will not fail to parse it.' The introductory theory/analogy for 'if statements' is completely missing.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with an engaging introduction explaining the concept of if statements, with a real-world analogy (e.g., traffic light, alarm clock decision).

issues.description: starter.py for the challenge already contains the complete working solution code. It includes all the if-statement logic, the any() generator expression, and the final display logic. This defeats the purpose of a 'practice exercise'.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace starter.py with scaffolding that has TODO comments and empty sections for the student to fill in.

issues.description: Typo in starter.py: 'Contains at at least one number' — duplicated 'at'.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to 'Contains at least one number'.

findings.lessonTitle: if-else Statements: The Fork in the Road

issues.description: content/01-theory.md is a placeholder stub with the same generic text as lesson 03. The introductory theory/analogy for if-else statements is missing.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with an engaging introduction explaining the 'fork in the road' concept — e.g., 'you either take the bus OR walk' with a clear motivation for why else is useful.

issues.description: challenge.json has only 1 test case ('Code runs without errors'). Lessons 01-03 have 4 test cases each, providing better validation.
issues.suggestedFix: Add test cases to verify output contains 'Ticket Category:', 'Ticket Price:', and 'Enjoy the show!'.

issues.description: challenge.json has only 1 test case ('Code runs without errors'). More validation test cases would strengthen the challenge.
issues.suggestedFix: Add test cases for 'BMI Calculator' title, 'Category:' output, 'Recommendation:' output.

issues.description: Content uses inline HTML tags (li, pre, table, ol) in markdown files (01-theory.md, 03-theory.md, 05-key_point.md). This may not render correctly in all markdown renderers.
issues.suggestedFix: Consider using standard markdown syntax for lists and tables for better portability.

issues.description: content/05-key_point.md declares 'Module 3 Complete! 🎉' and lists all concepts mastered, but Lesson 07 (Pattern Matching) still exists in this module. This misleads the student into thinking the module is finished when there is one more lesson.
issues.suggestedFix: Change to 'Core Module 3 Complete!' or add a note like 'Up next: an optional advanced lesson on Python 3.10+ pattern matching.'

issues.description: challenge.json has only 1 test case. The Movie Ticket Pricing System challenge could benefit from more validation.
issues.suggestedFix: Add test cases for 'Movie Ticket Pricing' title, 'Age Category:', 'Ticket Price:' outputs.

issues.description: Content uses inline HTML extensively (ol, li, table, ul tags) in 01-theory.md and 03-theory.md. Rendering depends on the platform.

In content/04-warning.md, Warning #5 title says 'Guards Must Use Captured Variables' with the WRONG example comment 'Can't use external variables directly in guard'. This is misleading — external variables CAN be used in guards. The actual error is the missing capture pattern before 'if' (i.e., 'case if score > threshold:' has no pattern).
issues.suggestedFix: Change warning title to 'Guards Require a Capture Pattern' and update the comment to explain that 'case if ...' is invalid syntax — a pattern (like a capture variable) is needed before the guard.

issues.description: The version reference 'Python 3.10+' is accurate (PEP 634, Python 3.10, Oct 2021). However, the lesson doesn't mention that Python 3.10 is now mature and widely available (current is 3.14). A brief note about version adoption could help students.
issues.webResearchNote: Python 3.10 released Oct 2021. match/case (PEP 634) is stable and unchanged through Python 3.14. The '3.10+' reference is fully accurate.
